fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

GILL J

These proceedings concern X, born in 2013, and is now aged 12. The applicant is the paternal grandmother. The respondents, who are not participating, are Ms Dodson, who is the mother, and Ms Avery, a transgender female who is referred to as the paternal parent in those proceedings.
X is a child with high needs and is highly vulnerable. She has extremely hindered physical development, for example her body mass index sits in the point-fourth percentile. She has a mild intellectual disability, she suffers from oppositional defiance disorder, she has a trauma history, a neglect history, potentially is autistic and / or has ADHD. X also has strong orthodontic needs. X struggles functioning as seen in multiple school suspensions.
X lived her early years in Western Australia in the primary care of the mother, but with the involvement of broader family, both the maternal family and the paternal family. That involvement included the paternal grandmother. The nature of the paternal grandmother’s involvement in X’s life altered when the paternal grandmother moved from Western Australia to Town D in New South Wales.
However, in September 2022 X came into the paternal grandmother’s care. At that point she was physically hurting herself, she was unable to engage in self-care, she was malnourished and underweight, she was described as feral and would hiss at people, conduct that was indicative of at least neglect. X exhibited violence and was unable to emotionally regulate herself. The paternal grandmother engaged strongly with X and with the difficulties that X was encountering, assisting in the engagement of X with educational and health services much to her benefit. Accordingly, the parties were able to enter into final consent orders in August 2024 that provided that X should continue to live with the paternal grandmother. Those orders also provided for regular return of X to Western Australia in order to spend time with her mother. As a result, it meant that X was living in Town D in proximity but in irregular contact with the paternal parent who struggles with their own mental health issues. The orders required cooperation between the parents and the paternal grandmother in long-term decision-making for X.
Shortly thereafter further risk issues emerged, in particular, risk issues involving the mother and as a result the paternal grandmother commenced proceedings in Western Australia to change the final order regime, such proceedings being transferred to this Court.
Those risk issues can be seen in the paternal parent’s allegations that in the period of December 2024 to January 2025 following X’s visit to her mother, X made sexual abuse allegations of the mother to the paternal parent. The nature of those allegations included high level sexual abuse. The issue of sexual abuse risk comes from those asserted reports to the paternal parent, comments that X has now made to the Child Impact Report writer, child welfare records that demonstrate that X has in the past been exposed to the mother’s partner who is a child sex offender, and limited assertions by X to New South Wales police. That is in addition to the neglect risk seen in the circumstances of X’s entry into the paternal grandmother’s care and in the records produced by the welfare agency. X is also at some risk of exposure to harm from the paternal parent due to the paternal parent’s poorly controlled mental health seen in the Child Impact Report to evidence neglect, physical harm and potential emotional harm. I will refer to the Child Impact Report further in a moment.
Since the production of the Child Impact Report the paternal parent has disengaged from the proceedings by consent saying there should be no further court documents served upon them. That discontinuance occurs in circumstances where the paternal parent has severe mental disorder, ADHD, a history of substance abuse, borderline personality disorder, autism, and has experienced both suicidal and homicidal thoughts. The mother too has ceased participating in the proceedings although in a less formal manner. The maternal grandmother who was initially a party to the proceedings withdrew. The parties were placed on notice on 17 December 2025 that the proceedings would go ahead on an undefended basis if they did not participate and that is what has happened.
A Child Impact Report was prepared and released on 28 November 2025. That Child Impact Report involved X, the paternal grandmother, the paternal parent and the mother. X expressed clearly in that report that she wants to remain in the paternal grandmother’s care forever, but that she also wants to have contact with her parents. She described the paternal parent as volatile to the extent that when she spends time with the paternal parent she wears a helmet to avoid being injured by the paternal parent throwing items. X also reported to the report writer that the mother had touched her inappropriately “down there” but without elaborating. X told the report writer that the mother had been in a relationship with a pervert who touched people and described exposure to drawings where he did “dirt” with young children. X further described being exposed to violent acts being perpetrated on her mother causing her bodily injury. X described neglect by the mother. The report writer identifies significant consequences related to the potential sexual abuse, neglect and other risks posed by the mother and risks posed by the paternal parent. These risks include a risk of serious ongoing harm particularly in the realm of X’s development. Neither parent offers, absent supervision, safe benefits for X. Each poses an unacceptable risk of harm if X was to be in their uncontrolled care. In contrast the paternal grandmother demonstrates a strong understanding of X’s needs and a strong commitment to meeting them. This is exemplified for example in the choice that she has made to relocate with X to the Region F with its tangible health and education access benefits, for example giving X better access to specialised schooling and to orthodontic care. It is also seen in the paternal grandmother’s persistent pursuit of NDIS support for X and in her follow-up and transfer of resources for X for example in respect of sexual assault counselling.
However, the capacity demonstrated by the paternal grandmother has also been demonstrated to have been hindered by imposing a consultation requirement upon her. The orders previously required her to consult with the parents before making a long-term decision. The evidence tendered in the proceedings shows that that is unworkable. X’s welfare requires parental responsibility to be exercised solely by the paternal grandmother. The paternal grandmother proposes that she give notice of a decision regarding X’s long-term well-being to the parents following the making of such a decision. Such a proposal is appropriate because it will assist the parents to have a better understanding of X’s life to enable, potentially in the future, appropriate engagement.
In conclusion, the orders proposed by the paternal grandmother are largely appropriate to X’s well-being. It is appropriate that she exercise sole parental responsibility, it is appropriate that she determine whether international travel take place, it is appropriate that she hold X’s passports, it is appropriate that X live with her and that there be limited arrangements for time with the parents that are safe in that they provide for electronic communication to take place and they provide for supervised time to take place. It is appropriate too that injunctions are made to protect X from potential exposure to an undermining of the fragile relationships that she enjoys with her parents and the important relationship that she enjoys with the paternal grandmother. However, those injunctions should be amended to enable discussion as determined appropriate by the paternal grandmother with X about the highly adverse experiences that she has had with her parents. Although the paternal grandmother proposed an order that might cater for future international travel with the parents this seems purely hypothetical and should not be made.
The orders should however include an ability for the paternal grandmother to apply for a passport should she need to do so for X.
I note that the Independent Children’s Lawyer (the “ICL”) has not seen X, there are exceptional circumstances that justify such a decision. X has been subject to interview with multiple agencies including multiple interviews about sexual assault. She has been interviewed by the report writer. X’s views are not the subject of a dispute before me today. The proceedings are conducted on an undefended basis. The decision made by the ICL not to meet with X is protective of a vulnerable young person protecting her from overexposure to systems and the impact of systems upon her. Such exposure could not be seen to bring a benefit of such significance as to justify such exposure. I am satisfied, as required by s 68LA(5D) that circumstances exist to justify the ICL not performing the duty of meeting with X.
